summary:
Acetazolamide reduces intraocular pressure primarily via its action on respiratory compensation which lowers blood CO₂ levels; this leads to improved aqueous humor outflow and reduced eye pressure.
```
The summary focuses only on the critical aspects related to how acetazolamide works clinically for treating angle-closure glaucoma rather than delving into detailed biochemical mechanisms or secondary physiological responses not directly linked to primary therapeutic benefit against elevated intraocular pressure.
``` ```plaintext
Acetazolamide decreases intraocular pressure mainly by increasing breathing rate, reducing blood CO₂ levels, and improving fluid drainage from the eyes.
This results in lower eye pressure due to better functioning of the trabecular meshwork.
``` Summary captures the essential mechanism driving the effectiveness of acetazolamide in managing angle-closure glaucoma while keeping within the two-sentence limit.
